elements.sent-2411-001: We present NEOWISE observations of Jupiter family comet 289P/Blanpain, the parent body of the Phoenicid meteoroid stream.
elements.sent-2411-002: Near-infrared images at 3.4$\micron$~($W1$) and 4.6$\micron$~($W2$) were obtained near perihelion on two occasions: UT 2019-10-30 (inbound, heliocentric distance $R_{\rm h}$ = 1.20\,au) and UT 2020-01-11/12 (outbound, $R_{\rm h}$ = 1.01\,au).
elements.sent-2411-003: To assess faint activity, we establish constraints on dust production driven by the limited sublimating area of water ice, based on studies of the 1956 Phoenicids.
elements.sent-2411-004: The ejected dust mass is $M_{\rm d}$ = 4100\,$\pm$\,200\,kg (inbound) and 1700\,$\pm$\,200\,kg (outbound), respectively.
elements.sent-2411-005: The dust production rates are $Q_{\rm dust}$\,=\,0.01\,$-$\,0.02\,kg\,s$^{-1}$, corresponding to dust-to-gas production ratio 2\,$\leqslant\,f_{\rm dg}\,\leqslant$\,6.
elements.sent-2411-006: The resulting fractional active area, $f_{\rm A}$ = 3.8$\pm$1.9$\times10^{-5}$, is the smallest yet reported.
elements.sent-2411-007: The absence of 4.6$\micron$ ($W2$) excess suggests that 289P contains negligible amounts of CO$_2$ and CO.
elements.sent-2411-008: Time-resolved analysis of weighted mean of $W1$ and $W2$ magnitudes finds a distinctive peak amplitude in the light curve having a rotational period $P_{\rm rot}$\,=\,8.8536\,$\pm$\,0.3860\,hr, however, further verification is needed.
elements.sent-2411-009: The perihelion-normalized nongravitational acceleration, $\alpha_{\rm NG ^\prime$ = 3.1$\times$10$^{-6}$, is approximately an order of magnitude smaller than the trend observed for well-studied comets, consistent with weak outgassing.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Current dust production from 289P, regardless of plausible assumptions for particle size and distribution, is an order of magnitude too small to produce the Phoenicid stream within its $\sim$300\,year dynamical lifetime.
elements.sent-2411-011: This suggests another mass supply, probably in 1743$-$1819, rapid rotational destruction of a sub-km precursor body, resulting in fragments equaling the mass of an object with radius $\sim$\,100\,m.
elements.sent-2411-012: Comet 289P/Blanpain (hereafter 289P), formerly known as D/1819~W1, was discovered in 1819 November by J.-J.. Blanpain \cite[][]{Kronk2003comebook}\footnote{\url{https://cometography.com/pcomets/1819w1.html .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The comet was subsequently linked to the Phoenicid meteor shower\footnote{PHO/254 from IAU Meteor Data Center, Nomenclature \citep{JopekJenniskens11 as a potential parent body due to their orbital similarities (\citeauthor{Ridley1957}\,\citeyear{Ridley1957 ; reviewed in \citeauthor{Ridley1963}\,\citeyear{Ridley1963 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Independent discovery and following observations were made for about two months until 1820 January.
elements.sent-2411-015: Throughout this period, the comet appeared small, faint and without a tail or gas.
elements.sent-2411-016: The orbit was determined to have a period of about 5\,years (Jupiter-family type), placing 289P shortly after its perihelion at the time of observations.
elements.sent-2411-017: Nevertheless, it remained lost for nearly two centuries after these initial observations.
elements.sent-2411-018: In 2003 November, the near-Earth asteroid (NEA) 2003~WY$_{25}$ was discovered by the Catalina Sky Survey \citep{Ticha2003MPEC .}

elements.sent-2411-036: Launched on 2009 December 14 into a Sun-synchronous polar orbit, WISE was equipped with a 40\,cm telescope that employed beam splitters to simultaneously capture images at four infrared wavelength bands centered at 3.4$\micron$ ($W1$), 4.6$\micron$ ($W2$), 12$\micron$ ($W3$), and 22\,$\micron$ ($W4$), enabling a comprehensive all-sky survey.
elements.sent-2411-037: It started on 2010 January 7 and continued until 2010 September 29.
elements.sent-2411-038: As the cryogen reserve was exhausted, data acquisition terminated for $W3$ and $W4$.
elements.sent-2411-039: WISE further continued surveying in a post-cryogenic two-band survey mode until 2011 February 1, when it was placed in hibernation.
elements.sent-2411-040: The two-band survey ($W1$ and $W2$), subsequently named NEOWISE, resumed 2013 December 13 and continues as the NEOWISE-R reactivation project.
elements.sent-2411-041: NEOWISE employs a 1024~$\times$~1024 pixel HgCdTe focal-plane array detector (Teledyne Imaging Sensors) for both the $W1$ and $W2$ bands \citep{Wright2010AJ .}
elements.sent-2411-042: The detector exhibits a pixel scale of $2\arcsec.75$ pix$^{-1}$ and a field of view (FOV) of 47$\arcmin$$\times$47$\arcmin$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Consecutive pairs of frames, one for each band, are captured within the same FOV every 11\,seconds, with each frame having an exposure time of 7.7\,seconds.
elements.sent-2411-044: The NEOWISE mission data ($W1$ and $W2$ bands) are available through the NASA/IPAC Infrared Science Archive (IRSA\footnote{\url{https://irsa.ipac.caltech.edu/frontpage/ ).}}

elements.sent-2411-188: The gas and thermal emissions are observed in the $W2$ band and at longer wavelengths \citep{Bauer2015ApJ .}
